title: Variables: var, let, const
why: To store values with different reassignment and scope rules.
realWorld: Cart total variable changes; API base URL usually stays constant.
definition: var is function-scoped; let/const are block-scoped; const prevents reassignment.
howItWorks: JS creates bindings in scope. let/const live in temporal dead zone until initialized. const object properties can still mutate.
mistakes: Using var in loops with async callbacks
memoryTrick: const protects the box label, not the items inside the box.
fastRevision: Prefer const by default, let for reassignment, avoid var.

title: Scope and Hoisting
why: To decide where variables/functions are accessible.
realWorld: A variable inside checkout() should not leak globally.
definition: Scope is visibility; hoisting is JS preparing declarations before execution.
howItWorks: Function declarations are hoisted with body. var is hoisted as undefined. let/const are hoisted but unusable before initialization.
mistakes: Confusing scope chain with prototype chain
memoryTrick: JS scans the attendance list before class starts, but let/const students cannot answer

title: Closures
why: To remember outer variables after outer function finishes.
realWorld: A counter function keeps private count.
definition: A closure is a function plus access to its lexical environment.
howItWorks: Inner function retains references to outer variables, not copies. Used in callbacks, factories, private state, debounce.
memoryTrick: Closure is a backpack. Function carries variables from home.

title: Event Loop
why: To run async work without blocking main JS execution.
realWorld: Timer callback runs after current call stack is empty.
definition: Event loop coordinates call stack, task queues, and microtasks.
howItWorks: Sync code runs first. Promise microtasks usually run before timer macrotasks. Browser and Node have runtime-specific phases.
mistakes: Blocking event loop with heavy loops
memoryTrick: Promise is VIP queue; timeout is regular queue.

title: Promises and Async/Await
why: To write async code cleanly.
realWorld: Fetch user profile, then fetch orders.
definition: Promise represents future success/failure; async/await is syntax over promises.
howItWorks: async function returns a promise. await pauses that async function, not the entire JS thread.
memoryTrick: async/await is promise in kurta-pajama: same person, cleaner look.
fastRevision: Use Promise.all for independent parallel tasks.

title: Callbacks
why: To pass behavior into functions.
realWorld: Array map callback transforms each item.
definition: A callback is a function passed to another function to be called later.
howItWorks: Callbacks power events, timers, array methods, and older async APIs.
memoryTrick: Callback = call me back later. Simple.
fastRevision: Callbacks are foundation; promises improve async composition.

title: Prototype
why: To share methods across objects efficiently.
realWorld: All arrays share push/map from Array.prototype.
definition: Prototype is the object another object delegates property lookup to.
howItWorks: If property not found on object, JS checks prototype chain until null.
memoryTrick: Prototype is family WhatsApp group: shared methods live there.
fastRevision: Classes are syntactic sugar over prototypes.

title: this Keyword
why: To refer to calling context.
realWorld: obj.say() gets this=obj; arrow keeps outer this.
definition: this is determined by how a function is called, except arrows capture lexical this.
howItWorks: Default, implicit, explicit, new binding decide this. Arrow functions do not bind their own this.
mistakes: Assuming this means where function is written
code:
const obj={x:1, f(){return this.x}};
const g=obj.f; g(); // undefined in strict mode
memoryTrick: this is not address proof; it depends who called you.

title: Map, Filter, Reduce
why: To transform arrays declaratively.
realWorld: Cart totals: map prices, filter active, reduce sum.
definition: map transforms, filter selects, reduce accumulates.
howItWorks: They return new values and do not mutate original array unless callback mutates objects.
memoryTrick: map = change clothes, filter = bouncer, reduce = final bill.

title: Debouncing and Throttling
why: To control high-frequency events.
realWorld: Search box API call after typing stops; scroll handler once per interval.
definition: Debounce waits for silence; throttle runs at fixed intervals.
howItWorks: Both use timers/closures to reduce work and improve performance.
memoryTrick: Debounce = wait till person stops talking. Throttle = one reply every 1 sec.

title: DOM Events: Bubbling and Capturing
why: To control event propagation.
realWorld: Click button inside card; event can reach parent card.
definition: Capturing goes down, target fires, bubbling goes up.
howItWorks: addEventListener third option/useCapture controls phase; stopPropagation stops further travel.
memoryTrick: Event is a courier: parent -> child -> parent path.

title: Storage: localStorage, sessionStorage, Cookies
why: To persist client-side state.
realWorld: Theme in localStorage; auth session cookie; tab-specific wizard in sessionStorage.
definition: localStorage persists, sessionStorage per tab, cookies travel with requests.
howItWorks: Cookies can be HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite. localStorage is accessible to JS, so XSS can read it.
memoryTrick: Cookie is passport shown to server; localStorage is desk drawer JS can open.
fastRevision: For auth, prefer secure HttpOnly cookies when possible.

title: Virtual DOM
why: To make UI updates predictable and efficient.
realWorld: State changes in a todo app update only affected UI in real DOM.
definition: Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation React compares before updating browser DOM.
howItWorks: React renders components to a tree, diffs with previous tree, then commits necessary DOM changes.
memoryTrick: React checks the draft before editing the real notebook.

title: JSX
why: To write UI structure inside JS with expressions.
realWorld: composes UI with data.
definition: JSX is syntax that compiles to React element creation.
howItWorks: JSX allows JS expressions in braces and must return one parent element or fragment.
mistakes: Using if statement directly in JSX expression
memoryTrick: JSX is HTML-looking JavaScript, not HTML.
fastRevision: Use map for lists, ternary/&& for conditions.

title: Props and State
why: To pass data and manage changing UI.
realWorld: Parent passes user prop; child tracks input state.
definition: Props are external inputs; state is internal component memory.
howItWorks: Changing state triggers render. Props should be treated read-only by child.
memoryTrick: Props = parcel from parent; state = personal diary.

title: useEffect
why: To synchronize component with external systems.
realWorld: Fetch data, subscribe to socket, update document title.
definition: useEffect runs side effects after render based on dependency array.
howItWorks: React runs effect after paint; cleanup runs before re-run/unmount. Dependencies control when it re-runs.
memoryTrick: Effect = after-render side job, not a calculator.
fastRevision: Use for external sync, not normal calculations.

title: useMemo and useCallback
why: To avoid expensive recalculation or unstable references.
realWorld: Memoize filtered list; stable callback passed to memoized child.
definition: useMemo caches value; useCallback caches function reference.
howItWorks: They help when calculation is expensive or referential equality affects child renders.
memoryTrick: Memoization is a sticky note: reuse until inputs change.

title: useRef
why: To store mutable values without re-rendering.
realWorld: Focus input; store timer id; track previous value.
definition: useRef returns a stable object whose .current can change without render.
howItWorks: DOM refs are assigned after render. Updating ref does not trigger render.
mistakes: Using ref for visible UI data
Mutating ref and expecting UI update
memoryTrick: Ref is a secret pocket: React will not announce changes.
fastRevision: Use for DOM and mutable non-UI values.

title: Context API
why: To avoid prop drilling for shared values.
realWorld: Theme, auth user, locale.
definition: Context passes data through tree without manual props at every level.
howItWorks: Provider supplies value; consumers re-render when value identity changes.
mistakes: Putting rapidly changing state in one huge context
memoryTrick: Context is office announcement system.

title: Redux Basics
why: To manage predictable global state.
realWorld: Cart, auth, complex dashboards.
definition: Redux uses a store, actions, reducers, and predictable state updates.
howItWorks: UI dispatches action; reducer returns new state; subscribers update.
mistakes: Mutating state in old Redux reducers
Using Redux for local form input
memoryTrick: Redux is bank ledger: action receipt, reducer accountant.
fastRevision: Use when state transitions are complex/shared.

title: Re-rendering and Performance
why: To understand why UI updates and how to optimize.
realWorld: Parent state change may re-render child.
definition: Re-render means React calls component function again to compute UI.
howItWorks: Render is not always DOM update. Memo, keys, stable props, virtualization reduce unnecessary work.
mistakes: Unstable inline objects causing memo miss
memoryTrick: React is overprotective friend: one state changed, it checks the group.

title: Controlled vs Uncontrolled Components
why: To manage form data predictably.
realWorld: Controlled input value comes from React state.
definition: Controlled components are driven by state; uncontrolled read from DOM refs.
howItWorks: Controlled gives validation and instant UI logic; uncontrolled can be simpler/faster for simple forms.
memoryTrick: Controlled = React is driving; uncontrolled = DOM is driving.

title: React Router
why: To create client-side navigation.
realWorld: Dashboard route /users/:id renders user page without full reload.
definition: React Router maps URL to components in SPA.
howItWorks: Routes, params, nested routes, loaders/actions in modern versions support navigation/data patterns.
memoryTrick: URL is state too.
fastRevision: Frontend route protection is UX, backend auth is security.

title: CSR, SSR, SSG, ISR
why: To choose the right rendering strategy.
realWorld: Dashboard CSR; product page SSG/ISR; account page SSR.
definition: CSR renders in browser; SSR per request; SSG at build; ISR periodically regenerates static pages.
howItWorks: Next.js supports mixed rendering. Data freshness, SEO, personalization, and cost decide the strategy.
memoryTrick: Restaurant analogy: CSR cook at table, SSR cook per order, SSG pre-packed, ISR refresh

title: App Router
why: To structure routes with layouts and server components.
realWorld: app/dashboard/page.tsx plus layout.tsx for nested UI.
definition: App Router is file-system routing in app/ using React Server Components and layouts.
howItWorks: Special files page, layout, loading, error, route, middleware create UI/data/API boundaries.
mistakes: Putting client hooks in server components
Confusing route handlers with server actions
memoryTrick: Folders become URLs; special files become behavior.
fastRevision: New projects usually use App Router.

title: Server and Client Components
why: To reduce client JS and place work correctly.
realWorld: Product list rendered on server; Add to cart button client.
definition: Server Components render on server; Client Components run interactive code in browser.
howItWorks: Server components can fetch data and access backend resources; client components use hooks/events and require use client.
mistakes: Passing functions from server to client
code:
'use client'
export default function Button(){ const [n,setN]=useState(0); }
memoryTrick: Server component = chef kitchen; client component = waiter interacting with customer.

title: Metadata and SEO
why: To manage title, description, Open Graph, robots.
realWorld: Product page dynamic metadata for product name.
definition: Metadata API defines SEO metadata per route.
howItWorks: Static or dynamic metadata can be exported; Next merges metadata through route segments.
memoryTrick: Metadata is the business card of your page.
fastRevision: Use metadata API in App Router.

title: API Routes / Route Handlers
why: To build backend endpoints inside Next app.
realWorld: /api/login route validates credentials and sets cookie.
definition: Route handlers define HTTP methods for API endpoints in app router.
howItWorks: Export GET/POST etc from route.ts. Handle request, return Response/NextResponse.
code: export async function GET(){ return Response.json({ok:true}); }
memoryTrick: Small backend inside frontend house.
fastRevision: Good for BFF/light APIs; large APIs may use dedicated backend.

title: Middleware
why: To run logic before request reaches route.
realWorld: Redirect unauthenticated user from /dashboard.
definition: Middleware intercepts matching requests for auth, redirects, headers, rewrites.
howItWorks: Runs before cached content/routes. Keep it lightweight and edge-compatible.
mistakes: Doing heavy DB work in middleware
Trusting frontend middleware as full security
memoryTrick: Security guard at building gate, not the entire police department.

title: Next.js Caching
why: To control performance and freshness.
realWorld: Cache product data, no-store admin stats.
definition: Next caches fetches/routes depending on settings and revalidation.
howItWorks: Use cache options, revalidate, dynamic rendering, tags, and path revalidation depending on app needs.
memoryTrick: Cache is fridge food: fast, but check expiry.

title: Image Optimization and Deployment
why: To improve performance and production readiness.
realWorld: next/image serves responsive optimized images.
definition: Next provides image optimization and deploys well on Vercel/Node platforms.
howItWorks: Use Image component, environment variables, build output, and platform limits carefully.
memoryTrick: NEXT_PUBLIC means public billboard.
fastRevision: Use env vars safely; optimize assets.

title: Node.js Runtime and Single Thread
why: To run JavaScript outside browser for servers/tools.
realWorld: Express API handles many users with async I/O.
definition: Node is a JS runtime built for non-blocking I/O.
howItWorks: JavaScript runs on main thread; libuv/event loop/worker pool handle async I/O and some expensive tasks.
mistakes: Doing CPU loops in request handler
memoryTrick: One cashier, many pending orders, kitchen workers help.
fastRevision: Great for I/O; be careful with CPU-heavy tasks.

title: Streams and Buffers
why: To handle large data efficiently.
realWorld: Upload/download video without loading whole file into memory.
definition: Buffer stores binary chunks; stream processes data chunk by chunk.
howItWorks: Readable/writable/transform streams handle backpressure and memory efficiency.
memoryTrick: Stream = water pipe, not bucket.

title: Process, Thread Pool, Cluster
why: To understand production Node behavior.
realWorld: Cluster uses multiple CPU cores for web server workers.
definition: process gives runtime info; thread pool handles expensive async tasks; cluster forks processes.
howItWorks: UV thread pool handles fs/crypto/dns/zlib. Cluster/process managers scale across cores.
mistakes: Assuming one Node process uses all cores
memoryTrick: process is the app passport.

title: Express Middleware
why: To compose request handling.
realWorld: Auth middleware checks token before protected routes.
definition: Middleware is a function that runs between request and response.
howItWorks: Order matters. It can modify req/res, end response, or call next(). Error middleware has four args.
memoryTrick: Middleware is security + receptionist + clerk in a queue.

title: REST APIs
why: To expose resources over HTTP.
realWorld: GET /users/1, POST /orders.
definition: REST uses HTTP methods/status codes to operate on resources.
howItWorks: Design nouns for URLs, verbs via methods, status codes for outcomes, JSON body for data.
memoryTrick: URL is noun, method is verb.

title: Authentication, Error Handling, Rate Limiting, Security
why: To protect APIs and keep them reliable.
realWorld: Login sets cookie, API validates token, limiter blocks brute force.
definition: Backend must validate identity, inputs, errors, and request volume.
howItWorks: Use validation, auth middleware, centralized errors, rate limiting, CORS, helmet, logs, and secure secrets.
memoryTrick: Backend is bouncer. Frontend is signboard.

title: Documents and Collections
why: To model flexible JSON-like data.
realWorld: User document stores profile fields and preferences.
definition: MongoDB stores BSON documents inside collections.
howItWorks: Documents can have nested fields/arrays. Schema is flexible but app should still enforce structure.
memoryTrick: Mongo document = JSON with gym membership.

title: CRUD Queries
why: To create/read/update/delete documents.
realWorld: Find active users, update order status.
definition: CRUD is basic database operation set.
howItWorks: Use find, insertOne, updateOne, deleteOne with filters/operators.
memoryTrick: $set is surgical update; replacement is bulldozer.

title: Aggregation
why: To transform and analyze data in pipeline stages.
realWorld: Monthly revenue by category.
definition: Aggregation pipeline processes documents through stages like $match, $group, $sort.
howItWorks: Place $match early, project fields, group after filtering for performance.
memoryTrick: Aggregation is assembly line.

title: Indexing and Performance
why: To make queries fast.
realWorld: Index email for login lookup.
definition: Index is a data structure that speeds reads at write/storage cost.
howItWorks: Indexes help filters/sorts when query matches index prefix/selectivity.
mistakes: Wrong field order in compound index
memoryTrick: Index is book index: faster lookup, extra pages.
fastRevision: High-cardinality queried fields are good index candidates.

title: Embedding vs Referencing and Populate
why: To model relationships.
realWorld: Embed address; reference author in posts.
definition: Embed stores related data together; reference stores ids to separate docs.
howItWorks: Embed for one-to-few and read-together. Reference for many-to-many, large/changing data.
memoryTrick: Embed when family lives together; reference when they live in different cities.

title: Transactions
why: To update multiple documents atomically.
realWorld: Transfer wallet balance between users.
definition: Transaction groups operations so they commit or rollback together.
howItWorks: MongoDB supports multi-document transactions, but schema design should avoid unnecessary transactions when possible.
mistakes: Using transactions for simple single doc update
code:
const session = await mongoose.startSession();
await session.withTransaction(async()=>{/* updates */});
memoryTrick: Transaction = all-or-nothing shaadi package.

title: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY
why: To retrieve filtered sorted data.
realWorld: Find active users ordered by signup date.
definition: SELECT chooses columns, WHERE filters rows, ORDER BY sorts results.
howItWorks: SQL logical order: FROM/JOIN -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> ORDER BY -> LIMIT.
code: SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = true ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10;
memoryTrick: WHERE is entry gate before grouping.

title: GROUP BY and HAVING
why: To aggregate rows into groups.
realWorld: Revenue per city, only cities above 1 lakh.
definition: GROUP BY groups rows; HAVING filters groups.
howItWorks: Aggregate functions include COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX. HAVING runs after grouping.
code: SELECT city, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY city HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;
memoryTrick: WHERE filters people before party; HAVING filters parties after headcount.
fastRevision: WHERE before group, HAVING after group.

title: JOINs
why: To combine related tables.
realWorld: Users joined with orders.
definition: JOIN combines rows from tables using matching keys.
howItWorks: INNER returns matches; LEFT keeps left table even without match; RIGHT/FULL less common.
mistakes: Turning LEFT into INNER with WHERE on right table
code: SELECT u.name, o.total FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id;
memoryTrick: JOIN is matrimony bureau for tables.
fastRevision: LEFT JOIN keeps all left rows.

title: Subqueries and CTEs
why: To break complex logic into parts.
realWorld: Find users whose order total is above average.
definition: Subquery is query inside query; CTE names a temporary result with WITH.
howItWorks: Use for readability; optimizer may inline/materialize depending DB.
code: WITH totals AS (SELECT user_id, SUM(total) s FROM orders GROUP BY user_id) SELECT * FROM totals WHERE s>1000;
memoryTrick: CTE is a named rough-work page.

title: Indexes, Normalization, Transactions, ACID
why: To design reliable databases.
realWorld: Unique email index; transaction for payment.
definition: Index speeds lookup; normalization reduces duplication; ACID guarantees reliable transactions.
howItWorks: ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Transactions protect multi-step changes.
code:
BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance=balance-100 WHERE id=1; UPDATE accounts SET balance=balance+100 WHERE id=2;
COMMIT;
memoryTrick: ACID = bank should not say maybe.

title: Firebase
why: To build apps quickly with managed backend services.
realWorld: Chat app with Firebase Auth, Firestore, Storage, Hosting.
definition: Firebase is Google-backed backend platform for auth, databases, hosting, functions, storage.
howItWorks: Client SDKs connect to services; security rules protect database/storage; server SDKs handle admin tasks.
memoryTrick: Firebase is startup booster rocket. Great speed, but watch fuel meter.
fastRevision: Great for MVP/mobile, realtime, Google ecosystem.

title: Supabase
why: To build Postgres-backed apps with managed auth/realtime/storage.
realWorld: SaaS dashboard with relational data and SQL queries.
definition: Supabase is an open-source Firebase alternative built around Postgres.
howItWorks: It exposes database, auth, storage, edge functions, realtime, and row-level security policies.
memoryTrick: Supabase = Firebase but SQL wali feeling.
fastRevision: Great when relational data and SQL are important.

title: Python Data Structures
why: To choose the right container.
realWorld: List for ordered tasks, set for unique tags, dict for lookup.
definition: List ordered mutable; tuple ordered immutable; set unique; dict key-value.
howItWorks: Choose by access pattern: order, uniqueness, lookup, immutability.
mistakes: Using list for membership at scale
memoryTrick: List queue, tuple sealed lunchbox, set bouncer, dict phonebook.

title: Python OOP
why: To bundle data and behavior.
realWorld: User class with methods for permissions.
definition: Class creates objects with attributes and methods.
howItWorks: __init__ initializes instance. self refers to instance. Inheritance shares behavior.
memoryTrick: self is the object saying mera data.

title: Decorators, Generators, Iterators
why: To write reusable wrappers and memory-efficient iteration.
realWorld: Decorator checks auth; generator streams rows.
definition: Decorator wraps function; iterator yields next values; generator creates iterator with yield.
howItWorks: Decorators take function and return function. Generators pause/resume producing values lazily.
memoryTrick: Decorator = function jacket; generator = lazy snack dispenser.

title: Comprehensions, Exceptions, Modules
why: To write concise, safe, organized Python.
realWorld: [x*x for x in nums if x>0]; try DB call except error.
definition: Comprehensions build collections; exceptions handle failures; modules organize code.
howItWorks: Use try/except around expected failures, finally for cleanup, modules for reuse.
memoryTrick: Exception handling is seatbelt, not driving style.

title: Git Core
why: To track code changes safely.
realWorld: Feature branch, commits, merge PR to main.
definition: Git is distributed version control.
howItWorks: Working tree -> staging area -> commit history. Branches are pointers to commits.
code:
git checkout -b feature/login
git add .
git commit -m "Add login API"
git push origin feature/login
memoryTrick: Git is time machine; GitHub is collaboration office.
fastRevision: Small commits, clear PRs, no secrets.

title: GitHub Pull Requests
why: To review and merge code collaboratively.
realWorld: Open PR from feature branch to main.
definition: PR proposes code changes for review before merge.
howItWorks: CI checks, reviews, comments, and merge strategies protect main branch.
code:
PR title: Add JWT refresh flow
Description: what changed, tests, screenshots, risks.
memoryTrick: PR is not just code upload; it is communication.
fastRevision: Good PRs explain why, not just what.

title: JWT
why: To carry signed claims between client and server.
realWorld: After login, server issues token; API verifies signature.
definition: JWT is a signed token containing claims.
howItWorks: Header + payload + signature. Server verifies signature and expiry; do not put secrets in payload.
memoryTrick: JWT is movie ticket: checker validates ticket, does not call your home.

title: Cookies and Sessions
why: To keep authenticated state across requests.
realWorld: Server creates session id, browser sends cookie each request.
definition: Cookie stores small client data; session stores user state server-side or in session store.
howItWorks: HttpOnly prevents JS access, Secure requires HTTPS, SameSite reduces CSRF.
memoryTrick: Cookie is passport automatically shown at gate.
fastRevision: For web auth, secure cookies are usually strong choice.

title: Refresh Tokens and OAuth
why: To keep users logged in and allow third-party login.
realWorld: Google Login redirects user, app gets identity, creates local session.
definition: Refresh token gets new access token; OAuth delegates authorization to provider.
howItWorks: Use secure redirect URIs, state/PKCE, token rotation, and server-side validation.
mistakes: Putting refresh token in unsafe storage
code: POST /auth/refresh -> verify refresh token -> issue new access token
memoryTrick: Access token = day pass; refresh token = membership card.
fastRevision: Protect refresh token more than access token.

title: Web Security Basics
why: To stop common attacks.
realWorld: Prevent XSS in comments, SQL injection in login, CSRF on payments.
definition: Security is validating input, protecting sessions, limiting access, and reducing attack surface.
howItWorks: Use parameterized queries, escaping, CSP, secure cookies, CSRF protections, rate limits, HTTPS, secrets management.
memoryTrick: Security is not one lock; it is building design.

title: Deployment Basics
why: To ship app reliably.
realWorld: Deploy Next frontend to Vercel, API to Render/Fly, DB to managed Mongo/Postgres.
definition: Deployment moves code to production infrastructure.
howItWorks: Build app, set env vars, connect DB, run migrations, monitor logs, use HTTPS and domain/DNS.
code:
npm run build
# set DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET in platform env
memoryTrick: It works on my machine is not deployment strategy.
fastRevision: Production = env vars + logs + rollback + monitoring.

title: Load Balancer and Horizontal Scaling
why: To handle more users reliably.
realWorld: Traffic distributed across multiple Node servers.
definition: Load balancer routes requests to healthy backend instances.
howItWorks: Horizontal scaling adds machines; vertical scaling makes one machine bigger.
mistakes: Storing session only in one server memory
memoryTrick: Load balancer is traffic police.
section: System Design Basics for Full Stack

title: CDN and Caching
why: To reduce latency and backend load.
realWorld: Images/JS served from nearby CDN; API result cached in Redis.
definition: CDN caches static assets globally; cache stores reusable responses/data.
howItWorks: Set cache headers, TTL, invalidation rules, and use Redis for hot data/session/rate-limit counters.
memoryTrick: Cache is fridge: fast food, expiry needed.
fastRevision: Cache public/static safely; protect private data.
section: System Design Basics for Full Stack

title: Database Scaling
why: To support growing data and traffic.
realWorld: Add indexes, read replicas, sharding, partitioning.
definition: DB scaling improves capacity, reads, writes, and latency.
howItWorks: Start with schema/index/query optimization, then replicas, partitioning/sharding, queues, denormalization.
memoryTrick: Database is often the real boss fight.
section: System Design Basics for Full Stack

title: Monolith, Microservices, Queues
why: To organize backend complexity.
realWorld: Email sending through queue; payments as separate service later.
definition: Monolith is one deployable app; microservices split services; queue processes async work.
howItWorks: Monolith is simpler initially. Microservices help independent scaling but add network/devops complexity. Queues decouple slow work.
memoryTrick: Queue is waiting room for background tasks.
fastRevision: Start monolith; split only with clear pain.
section: System Design Basics for Full Stack
